description.task: Implement a SearchCaseTimer class that measures best, average, and worst case operation counts for linear search on an integer array. Best case: target is the first element. Worst case: target is the last element or not found. Average case: target is in the middle.
instructions: countWorstCase(n): build array of size n using buildArray(), set target = arr[n-1], loop counting ops until found, return count
countAverageCase(n): build array, set target = arr[n/2], loop counting ops until found, return count
Both methods must use the same counting pattern as countBestCase()
countBestCase always returns 1 for any n
description.hint: For countWorstCase: int target = arr[n-1]; then the same for-loop with ops++ and break on match. For average: target = arr[n/2];
description.expectedOutput:
n=10 | Best=1 | Average=6 | Worst=10
n=100 | Best=1 | Average=51 | Worst=100
n=1000 | Best=1 | Average=501 | Worst=1000

description.task: Implement a BubbleSortAnalyzer class for bubble sort that counts comparisons in three scenarios: best case (already sorted array — O(n) with early exit), worst case (reverse-sorted array — O(n²)), and average case (random-ish array).
instructions: bestCaseComparisons(n): create int[n] filled [1..n] (already sorted), call sortAndCount, return result
worstCaseComparisons(n): create int[n] filled [n..1] (reverse sorted), call sortAndCount, return result
Best case with optimized bubble sort = n-1 comparisons (one pass, no swaps)
Use sortAndCount() for both — do not write separate sorting logic
description.hint: For bestCase: int[] arr = new int[n]; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) arr[i]=i+1; return sortAndCount(arr). For worst: arr[i]=n-i;
description.expectedOutput:
n=5 | Best=4 | Worst=10
n=10 | Best=9 | Worst=45

description.task: Implement a BinarySearchAnalyzer class that counts comparisons for best, average, and worst cases in binary search. Best: target is exactly at mid on first iteration. Worst: target is not found or at a leaf. Average: target in the second half of the search range.
instructions: worstCase(n): build sorted array, search for -1 (not present), return comparison count
bestCase always returns 1 (middle element found immediately)
Do not modify bestCase() or binarySearchCount()
description.hint: int[] arr = buildSortedArray(n); return binarySearchCount(arr, -1); The target -1 is never present so the loop runs until lo > hi.
description.expectedOutput:
n=8 | Best=1 | Worst=4
n=16 | Best=1 | Worst=5
n=1024 | Best=1 | Worst=11
